q: What is the difference between a transformation and an action in PySpark, and why does Spark use lazy evaluation?
a: Transformations like filter, select, and join are lazy: they only add nodes to the DAG and read no data. Actions like collect, count, show, and write are eager and trigger the whole DAG to execute, which is the only point at which Spark actually reads and processes data. Lazy evaluation lets the Catalyst optimizer reorder and fuse stages, push predicates down to the scan, and prune unused columns before any I/O happens. Repeated actions replay the DAG from scratch unless you cache an intermediate DataFrame.
fs: Lazy evaluation lets Catalyst push predicates down, prune columns, and fuse stages before any byte is read.
fj: Forgetting that show() is an action, or thinking transformations execute immediately.

code.label: Lazy vs eager
lines: # nothing read yet: just a DAG node

followups.q: If actions replay the whole DAG, why not just cache everything to be safe?
followups.a: Caching costs memory and an eager materialization pass, so caching a single-use DataFrame is pure overhead that can evict genuinely reused blocks. Only `cache`/`persist` a DataFrame that feeds two or more actions, and `unpersist` once done. For a linear job with one terminal `write`, caching adds nothing.

followups.q: Is take(5) an action, and does it run the entire DAG like count()?
followups.a: `take(n)` and `show(n)` are actions but short-circuit: Spark runs one partition at a time and stops once it has `n` rows, often touching just a few partitions. `count()` must scan everything. So `take` is cheap for previewing but its result order is not deterministic across runs.

followups.q: Does explain() count as an action that reads data?
followups.a: No. `explain()` only compiles and prints the logical/physical plan from Catalyst without executing it, so it reads zero data. It is the safe way to inspect pushdown, join type, and shuffle count before paying for any I/O.

q: Classify filter, withColumn, union, groupBy().agg(), join, and sortBy as narrow or wide, and how do you avoid an unnecessary shuffle when joining a large table to a small lookup?
a: filter, withColumn/map, and union are narrow: each input partition feeds at most one output partition with no network transfer. groupBy().agg(), join, and sortBy are wide because matching keys must co-locate, forcing a shuffle that writes to disk, sends over the network, and deserializes, often 10-100x slower than narrow ops. To join a large table to a small lookup, broadcast the small DataFrame so it is copied to every executor and the large table never moves. Spark auto-broadcasts when the small side is under spark.sql.autoBroadcastJoinThreshold, and explain() will show BroadcastHashJoin.
fs: Use broadcast() on the small table to eliminate the shuffle of the large table entirely.
fj: Letting Spark default to a sort-merge join that shuffles both tables instead of broadcasting the small side.

code.label: Broadcast the small lookup
lines: # small side copied to every executor

followups.q: union is narrow, yet why does df.union(df2).distinct() still trigger a shuffle?
followups.a: `union` itself just concatenates partitions with no network movement, so it is narrow. But `distinct` (like `groupBy`) must compare rows across the whole dataset, which forces a hash shuffle. The shuffle comes from the dedup, not the `union` that precedes it.

followups.q: Why is a wide shuffle 10-100x slower than a narrow op beyond just network cost?
followups.a: A shuffle writes every partition's output to local disk as shuffle files, fetches them over the network, then deserializes and re-sorts on the receiving side. That is disk I/O plus serialization plus network plus a stage boundary that blocks pipelining, so even a fast network leaves the disk and serde costs dominating.

followups.q: When would broadcasting the small side actually be the wrong choice?
followups.a: When the small side is not small in memory: the broadcast estimate is on deserialized in-memory size, so a 50MB compressed Parquet can balloon past executor heap. It is also wrong if the lookup grows over time near the threshold, since silent flips to sort-merge join hurt predictability. Verify with `explain`.

q: What is the difference between repartition(1) and coalesce(1) when writing a single Parquet file, and which would you choose?
a: coalesce(n) is a narrow transformation that merges partitions locally with no shuffle, making it cheap for reducing partitions, though it can leave uneven partition sizes. repartition(n) is a wide transformation that does a full shuffle to produce evenly sized partitions, which is more expensive. To write a single file, prefer coalesce(1) since it avoids the unnecessary shuffle and the single-file write is the bottleneck anyway, but watch for OOM since one executor must hold the whole dataset. Increasing partitions with repartition is right before a heavy join on a skewed key or after reading a single flat file that yields too few partitions, targeting roughly 128-256 MB per partition.
fs: Reducing partitions uses coalesce (no shuffle); rebalancing or increasing uses repartition (full shuffle, even sizes).
fj: Using repartition(1) to write a single file, paying for a full shuffle when coalesce(1) suffices.

followups.q: A surprising gotcha: how can coalesce(10) actually slow down the upstream computation?
followups.a: `coalesce` pushes its narrow nature up the lineage: collapsing to 10 output partitions can force the preceding stage (e.g. a heavy map) to run with only 10 tasks, throttling parallelism for the whole computation. When you need both upstream parallelism and few output files, use `repartition` instead, accepting its shuffle.

followups.q: How do you write fewer large files without coalesce(1) forcing one executor to hold everything?
followups.a: Pick a coalesce/repartition target that yields ~128-256MB files rather than 1, e.g. `coalesce(8)` for a 1GB result. Or set `spark.sql.files.maxRecordsPerFile` so each task caps file size. Forcing a single file is rarely worth the OOM risk and lost write parallelism.

followups.q: Does coalesce(1) guarantee a single output file?
followups.a: Yes for the data part, but the directory still gets `_SUCCESS` and possibly `_committed`/`_started` metadata files. If you partitionBy as well, you get one file per partition directory, not one overall. `coalesce(1)` controls task count, not the commit-protocol sidecar files.

q: Explain the mechanical difference between a Broadcast Hash Join and a Sort-Merge Join, and how would you fix a slow 600 GB to 400 MB join?
a: A Sort-Merge Join shuffles both sides by the join key, sorts each partition, then merges, which means two full shuffles and is expensive even at moderate scale. A Broadcast Hash Join collects the small table on the driver, broadcasts it to every executor as a local hash map, and lets large-table rows probe it locally with no shuffle of the big table, often 5-20x faster but risking OOM if the broadcast side exceeds executor memory. The safest fix is an explicit broadcast(products) hint, verified with explain(mode='formatted') showing BroadcastHashJoin. You can alternatively raise spark.sql.autoBroadcastJoinThreshold (default 10 MB) if executors have enough memory, or rely on AQE to auto-convert SMJ to BHJ at runtime.
fs: An explicit broadcast() hint is the safest fix and needs no threshold change; verify with explain().
fj: Raising autoBroadcastJoinThreshold without checking that executors have memory for the broadcast table, causing OOM.

followups.q: The 400 MB side is too big to broadcast but a sort-merge join is too slow; what is your next option?
followups.a: Use a shuffle-hash join or pre-bucket both tables on `product_id` with matching bucket counts so the join needs no shuffle. Alternatively pre-shuffle once and `cache` the partitioned big side if it is reused. If 400MB exceeds heap, broadcasting is simply off the table and bucketing is the durable fix.

followups.q: Why does the driver become a bottleneck in a broadcast hash join?
followups.a: The small side is collected to the driver first, then shipped to every executor via the broadcast variable. So driver heap must hold the full deserialized table, and the broadcast serialization happens once on the driver. A too-large broadcast OOMs the driver before any executor even sees it.

followups.q: How does AQE decide at runtime to flip a sort-merge join to a broadcast?
followups.a: After the shuffle stage materializes, AQE reads the actual shuffle-write bytes of each side. If one side's real size falls under `spark.sql.adaptive.autoBroadcastJoinThreshold`, it rewrites the join to BHJ for the remaining plan, fixing cases where compile-time row estimates were too pessimistic.

q: How do you use PySpark window functions to compute a per-user running total, rank by amount, and lag of the previous transaction, and do they shuffle?
a: Define a WindowSpec with Window.partitionBy("user_id") plus an orderBy, using one spec ordered by date and another ordered by amount descending. Running total is sum("amount").over(w_date) with the default ROWS UNBOUNDED PRECEDING frame, rank is rank().over(w_amt), and the previous amount is lag("amount", 1).over(w_date) which is null for the first row per user. partitionBy causes a shuffle because rows with the same user must co-locate, making it a wide transformation, but unlike groupBy().agg() it does not collapse rows. Use dense_rank for no gaps after ties or row_number for a unique sequence.
fs: Window functions shuffle via partitionBy but produce one output row per input row rather than collapsing them.
fj: Confusing rank, dense_rank, and row_number, or assuming windows reduce rows like a groupBy aggregation.

followups.q: What is the difference between a ROWS frame and a RANGE frame, and why can it change your running total?
followups.a: `ROWS` counts physical rows around the current row; `RANGE` groups by the ordering value, so all rows sharing the same `date` get the same cumulative sum. An ordered window with an aggregate defaults to RANGE UNBOUNDED PRECEDING, which can surprise you on tie values. Use an explicit `rowsBetween` frame for true row-by-row totals.

followups.q: What happens to performance if you partitionBy nothing, e.g. Window.orderBy('date') alone?
followups.a: With no `partitionBy`, Spark must put every row into a single partition to compute the global order, so one task processes the entire dataset and almost certainly OOMs or spills hard. A warning is even logged. Global-order windows are an anti-pattern at scale; always partition.

followups.q: Can you reuse one WindowSpec for both running total and lag if their frames differ?
followups.a: Yes for the partition/order, but the frame matters: `lag`/`rank`/`row_number` ignore the frame clause, while aggregate functions like `sum` honor it. So one `Window.partitionBy(...).orderBy(...)` is fine for `lag`, but if you want a bounded running sum, add an explicit `rowsBetween` only on the aggregate's `over`.

q: How would you write a PySpark ETL job to deduplicate clickstream events on a natural key, fill NULL session_id, and number events within each session?
a: Use dropDuplicates(["user_id", "event_ts", "event_type"]) on the natural key rather than distinct(), which compares all columns and keeps rows differing only in noise fields. Fill NULL session_id with when(col("session_id").isNull(), concat(user_id, '_', date(event_ts))).otherwise(col("session_id")). Then add row_number() over a window partitioned by session_id ordered by event_ts, a wide shuffle operation. On write, size partitions to roughly 128-256 MB and use partitionBy on a column like event_type so downstream jobs prune irrelevant files.
fs: dropDuplicates on a subset key preserves legitimate differences in other columns, unlike distinct().
fj: Using distinct() instead of dropDuplicates(subset), which fails to dedupe rows that differ only in session_id.

followups.q: dropDuplicates keeps an arbitrary row among duplicates; how do you keep the LATEST one deterministically?
followups.a: `dropDuplicates` picks a nondeterministic survivor, which breaks reruns. Instead use `row_number()` over `partitionBy(key).orderBy(desc('event_ts'))` and filter `rn == 1`. That deterministically keeps the newest record per key and is reproducible across runs and node failures.

followups.q: Why fill session_id BEFORE the row_number window rather than after?
followups.a: The window partitions by `session_id`; if you number first, all the NULL-session rows collapse into one giant partition and get numbered together, mixing unrelated users. Filling synthetic session ids first ensures each real session gets its own correct sequence.

followups.q: Will dropDuplicates on event_ts catch true duplicate clicks if timestamps have millisecond jitter?
followups.a: No. If the same logical click arrives with slightly different `event_ts`, an exact-match key misses it. You would need to truncate the timestamp to a coarser bucket or dedupe on a stable event id. Exact-key dedup only works when the key is genuinely identical across duplicates.

q: A join runs 6 hours with one task stuck while 199 finish in minutes; how do you diagnose and fix the data skew?
a: One straggler task with massive shuffle read is the classic data-skew symptom: a single hot join key such as 'guest' or NULL dominates, so all its records land in one partition. Manual salting fixes this by appending a random bucket index 0..N to the skewed key in the large table and replicating each small-table row across all N buckets via explode, so the hot key splits across N partitions. Alternatively, enable AQE with spark.sql.adaptive.enabled and spark.sql.adaptive.skewJoin.enabled, which detects skewed partitions at runtime and splits them with zero code change on Spark 3.0+. AQE is the preferred first line of defense, while salting gives finer control, works on Spark 2.x, and avoids writing the skewed shuffle in the first place at the cost of N-times fan-out on the small table.
fs: A single straggler task with outsized shuffle read means skew on one hot join-key value, fixable with salting or AQE skewJoin.
fj: Blaming the cluster or general slowness instead of recognizing one hot key, and ignoring that salting multiplies the small table N times.

followups.q: AQE skewJoin is on, yet the straggler persists; what could prevent AQE from splitting it?
followups.a: AQE only splits a partition exceeding both `skewedPartitionFactor` x median AND `skewedPartitionThresholdInBytes`; a single mega-key may be too small in bytes to trip the threshold yet still slow. AQE also cannot split when the skewed side feeds certain join types or when there is no shuffle (e.g. already-broadcast). Then manual salting is required.

followups.q: If the hot key is NULL, what is a cheaper fix than salting?
followups.a: NULL keys never match in an inner join, so filter them out before the join and union them back if needed. For outer joins, split the NULL rows into a separate branch handled without a join. This sidesteps salting entirely because NULLs carry no join semantics.

followups.q: What is the downside of choosing a very large salt N like 256?
followups.a: The small side is replicated N times via `explode`, so N=256 multiplies its shuffle and join cost 256-fold, which can dwarf the savings on the big side. Pick N just large enough to spread the hottest key below the partition target, often 8-32, not arbitrarily high.

q: How would you use the Spark UI to diagnose a 3-hour job that should take 15 minutes, and fix the common root causes?
a: Start at the Jobs tab sorted by duration, drill into the slow job's Stages tab sorted by duration, then open the worst stage's Summary Metrics to compare median versus max task duration. Max far exceeding median signals data skew; non-zero Shuffle Spill (Disk) signals executors out of memory; tiny per-task input sizes signal too many small partitions. Fix skew with AQE skewJoin or broadcast() of the small side, fix spill by raising spark.sql.shuffle.partitions or executor memory so partitions fit in memory, and fix small partitions with AQE coalescePartitions or a manual coalesce. The guiding target is 128-256 MB per partition, which AQE handles dynamically when adaptive execution is enabled.
fs: Compare max versus median task duration in Summary Metrics to confirm skew, and check Shuffle Spill (Disk) for memory pressure.
fj: Guessing at fixes without navigating Jobs to Stages to Summary Metrics, and ignoring the per-task input size that reveals small partitions.

followups.q: Spill (Memory) is huge but Spill (Disk) is small; should you worry?
followups.a: Spill (Memory) is the in-memory size of data that spilled; Spill (Disk) is its compressed on-disk footprint. A large memory-spill with small disk-spill still means tasks could not fit working data in heap and paid serialization plus disk I/O. Both indicate memory pressure; raise partitions or executor memory.

followups.q: Tasks are fast but the job still drags between stages; where do you look?
followups.a: Check the Event Timeline and the gap between stage completion and the next stage start, plus the driver. Long gaps often mean driver-side work: huge plan compilation, listing millions of files, or a `collect`/broadcast on the driver. Per-task metrics look fine because the bottleneck is not in the executors.

followups.q: GC time shows as a large fraction of task time; what does that tell you?
followups.a: Heavy GC means executors are thrashing the heap, usually from too-few large partitions, caching too much, or skew packing one task. Reduce data per task by raising `shuffle.partitions`, lower cache footprint with a `_SER` storage level, or fix skew. It is a symptom of memory pressure, not a tuning knob itself.

q: How do you design an end-to-end partition strategy to bring an 800 GB daily PySpark pipeline from 90 minutes under a 20-minute SLA?
a: Fix the shuffle first since it has the biggest impact: the default 200 shuffle partitions give 4 GB each at 800 GB and guarantee spill, so set spark.sql.shuffle.partitions to roughly 3200 to target about 256 MB per partition. Enable AQE with coalescePartitions and skewJoin to coalesce undersized partitions and handle residual skew automatically. Tune source reads via spark.sql.files.maxPartitionBytes around 128 MB and merge tiny files, and for output repartition by the write partition key before partitionBy("region") so each region gets evenly sized roughly 256 MB files instead of many tiny ones. Apply changes in order, measuring stage durations in the Spark UI after each, and add repartition hints on join keys only if AQE does not auto-handle it.
fs: Set shuffle.partitions to target ~256 MB per partition first since the default 200 causes spill at 800 GB scale.
fj: Leaving spark.sql.shuffle.partitions at the default 200 and writing partitionBy(region) without repartitioning, producing spill and tiny files.

followups.q: If AQE coalescePartitions is on, why bother setting shuffle.partitions to 3200 at all?
followups.a: AQE can only coalesce DOWN from the configured partition count; it never adds partitions. If you leave it at 200, each task still starts with 4GB and spills before AQE can act. Set a high static count so the initial split is small enough, then let AQE merge undersized ones back up to ~256MB.

followups.q: You hit the 20-minute SLA but the output has thousands of tiny region files; what went wrong?
followups.a: `partitionBy('region')` with many shuffle tasks means each task writes one file per region, so N tasks x M regions = huge file count. The `repartition('region')` before write fixes it by collocating each region in one task. Without it, you trade SLA for a small-file problem downstream.

followups.q: Why measure after each change instead of applying all tunings at once?
followups.a: Tunings interact and can mask each other: fixing the shuffle might eliminate the spill that you would otherwise mis-attribute to memory, and over-raising partitions can add scheduling overhead. Changing one knob and reading stage durations isolates which change actually moved the needle, avoiding cargo-cult config.

q: Given orders(order_id, seller_id, amount), compute total revenue per seller ordered by revenue descending with ties by seller_id, assigned to result?
a: Group orders by seller_id and aggregate F.sum("amount") with a clean alias of total_revenue. Then order by F.desc("total_revenue") followed by seller_id ascending so ties resolve deterministically. Assign the final DataFrame with columns seller_id and total_revenue to a variable named result, since the runner collects and diffs it. Deterministic ordering matters because the grader compares row order.
fs: Order by desc revenue then seller_id ascending so ties are deterministic and the diff matches.
fj: Omitting the seller_id tiebreaker, leaving non-deterministic ordering, or not assigning to a variable named result.

followups.q: If some amounts are NULL, how does F.sum treat them and could that skew the ranking?
followups.a: `F.sum` ignores NULLs, so a seller with all-NULL amounts returns NULL (not 0), and a NULL total sorts unpredictably. Wrap with `F.coalesce(F.sum('amount'), F.lit(0))` if you need zero-revenue sellers ranked at the bottom deterministically rather than dropping or floating.

followups.q: The grader diffs row order; does a final orderBy guarantee that order survives the write?
followups.a: `orderBy` before `collect` holds, but writing to multiple files and re-reading does NOT preserve global order, and any later wide op re-shuffles. Keep the `orderBy` as the last step before the action the grader runs, and avoid a trailing `repartition`.

followups.q: Would a window sum be wrong here instead of groupBy?
followups.a: Not wrong, just wasteful: `sum().over(partitionBy('seller_id'))` keeps one row per order with the total repeated, so you would still need a `dropDuplicates`. `groupBy().agg()` collapses directly to one row per seller, which is exactly the required shape with a single shuffle.

q: Why is a Python UDF slow, and how do a pandas (vectorized) UDF and a native Spark expression each fix it?
a: A plain Python UDF runs row by row and forces Spark to serialize each row from the JVM to a Python worker and back, so Catalyst treats it as an opaque black box that blocks predicate pushdown and codegen. A pandas_udf is vectorized: Spark moves data in Apache Arrow columnar batches to the Python worker, the function operates on a whole pandas Series at once, and results return via Arrow, typically 3-100x faster. Best of all is staying in native Spark functions (when/otherwise, regexp_extract, etc.), which run entirely in the JVM with codegen and full optimizer support and never cross the Python boundary. Newer Spark also offers Arrow UDFs that skip the pandas conversion for another ~10% speedup and lower memory.
fs: Prefer native Spark expressions; if you must go to Python, use a vectorized pandas_udf with Arrow batches, never a row-at-a-time UDF.
fj: Reaching for a row-wise Python UDF for logic that a native when/otherwise or regexp expression could express in the JVM.

followups.q: Concretely, how does a Python UDF block predicate pushdown?
followups.a: Catalyst cannot see inside the Python function, so it cannot prove a filter on the UDF's output is safe to push to the scan, and it will not reorder a filter past it. A `df.filter(myUdf(col)>0)` therefore reads all rows and evaluates the UDF before filtering, instead of skipping row groups at the Parquet scan.

followups.q: Beyond raw speed, what reliability risk does a Python UDF add that a native expression does not?
followups.a: It spins up Python worker processes whose memory lives outside the JVM heap, counted against `spark.executor.pyspark.memory`/overhead. A heavy UDF can OOM the container via off-heap growth that executor heap tuning will not catch, and a Python exception kills the task. Native expressions have neither failure mode.

followups.q: If logic truly needs Python, what makes a pandas_udf faster than even an Arrow-optimized row UDF?
followups.a: A `pandas_udf` operates on a whole `pd.Series`/batch, so the per-row Python interpreter overhead is amortized and vectorized numpy ops run in C. An Arrow row UDF still calls Python once per row; it only speeds up the serialization layer. Vectorization, not serde, is the bigger win when the op is naturally columnar.

q: When is a pandas_udf the right tool over a native expression, and what gotchas come with grouped-map (applyInPandas)?
a: Use a pandas_udf when the logic genuinely cannot be expressed in native Spark functions, for example calling a scikit-learn model, a scipy routine, or complex per-group pandas logic. A SERIES-to-SERIES pandas_udf vectorizes a scalar transform over Arrow batches; for whole-group logic use applyInPandas (grouped map), but it materializes each entire group as one pandas DataFrame in the worker, so a skewed group can OOM that executor. Control batch size with spark.sql.execution.arrow.maxRecordsPerBatch and ensure pyarrow is installed and spark.sql.execution.arrow.pyspark.enabled is true. Always declare an explicit return schema, because applyInPandas cannot infer it.
fs: applyInPandas loads a whole group into one pandas DataFrame on the worker, so a hot group can OOM; cap batch size and watch skew.
fj: Assuming pandas_udf is always faster, or using applyInPandas on skewed groups and blaming Spark when an executor OOMs.

followups.q: Does maxRecordsPerBatch help with an applyInPandas OOM on a hot group?
followups.a: No. `maxRecordsPerBatch` only chunks scalar `pandas_udf` and Arrow transfer batches; `applyInPandas` must hand the function the ENTIRE group at once because the logic is per-group. The only fixes for a hot group are pre-aggregation, splitting the group, or more executor memory.

followups.q: What goes wrong if the pandas DataFrame you return doesn't match the declared schema?
followups.a: Spark coerces columns positionally/by name to the declared schema; a missing column, wrong dtype, or extra column raises at runtime or silently nulls/casts. Float-vs-int and timezone-naive timestamps are classic mismatches. Always build the return frame with exactly the declared column names, order, and dtypes.

followups.q: When would mapInPandas be a better fit than applyInPandas?
followups.a: `mapInPandas` streams arbitrary-sized batches of the DataFrame without grouping, so it avoids holding a whole group in memory and is ideal for per-row enrichment or batched model inference. Choose `applyInPandas` only when the logic genuinely needs all rows of a key together (e.g. per-group model fit).

q: What does cache() actually do, how does it differ from persist(), and which storage level fits a large dataset reused many times?
a: cache() is shorthand for persist(MEMORY_AND_DISK), the default DataFrame level: it keeps deserialized partitions in memory and spills evicted blocks to disk rather than recomputing. persist() lets you pick a StorageLevel: MEMORY_ONLY (fast but recomputes on eviction), MEMORY_AND_DISK_SER (serialized, smaller footprint, more CPU), or DISK_ONLY (smallest memory, slowest). Both are lazy, so nothing is cached until the next action materializes it, and you should unpersist() when done to free the blocks. For a large dataset reused many times that does not fit in RAM, MEMORY_AND_DISK_SER trades CPU for a smaller footprint and avoids recomputation; only cache when a DataFrame is reused, since a single-use DataFrame gains nothing.
fs: cache() == persist(MEMORY_AND_DISK); it is lazy, only pays off on reuse, and you should unpersist() to release blocks.
fj: Caching a DataFrame used once, or expecting cache() to take effect before the first action triggers it.

followups.q: Note that the DataFrame cache() default differs from the RDD default; why does that matter?
followups.a: For RDDs `cache()` is `MEMORY_ONLY`, but for DataFrames/Datasets it is `MEMORY_AND_DISK`. So a DataFrame silently spills to disk instead of recomputing on eviction, which is usually what you want but means cached DataFrames can consume disk you did not expect. Knowing the level avoids surprise disk pressure.

followups.q: If a cached DataFrame's source files change underneath, what happens on the next action?
followups.a: The cache is a point-in-time materialization, so subsequent actions serve stale data even after the underlying files change; Spark will not detect the change. You must `unpersist` (or recreate) to pick up new data. This is a common source of 'why is my output stale' bugs.

followups.q: Why might MEMORY_ONLY actually be slower than MEMORY_AND_DISK for a barely-too-big dataset?
followups.a: With `MEMORY_ONLY`, partitions that do not fit are dropped and RECOMPUTED from lineage on every access, so a dataset slightly over heap thrashes recomputation. `MEMORY_AND_DISK` spills the overflow once and reads it back, which is far cheaper than re-running the whole upstream DAG repeatedly.

q: What does Adaptive Query Execution (AQE) do at runtime, and which three optimizations does it apply?
a: AQE re-optimizes the physical plan using actual shuffle statistics gathered at each stage boundary, instead of relying only on stale compile-time estimates. Its three core features are: coalescing post-shuffle partitions so many tiny partitions merge into right-sized ones (controlled by advisoryPartitionSizeInBytes), converting a sort-merge join to a broadcast hash join when a side turns out smaller than the adaptive broadcast threshold, and skew-join handling that splits and replicates oversized partitions into even tasks. It is enabled by default since Spark 3.2 via spark.sql.adaptive.enabled, and you also keep spark.sql.adaptive.skewJoin.enabled on. AQE is why you can set a high static shuffle.partitions and let Spark coalesce down to the right number per stage.
fs: AQE re-plans at shuffle boundaries: coalesce partitions, SMJ-to-broadcast, and skew-split, using real runtime stats.
fj: Thinking AQE replaces tuning entirely, or not knowing it has been on by default since Spark 3.2.

followups.q: Why does AQE only re-optimize at shuffle (stage) boundaries and not continuously?
followups.a: Exact statistics (per-partition byte sizes, row counts) only become known after a shuffle stage fully materializes its map output. AQE uses those concrete stats to re-plan the remaining stages. Between boundaries there is no new ground truth, so re-planning mid-stage would just re-guess. Shuffle-less queries get no AQE benefit.

followups.q: Can AQE ever make a query slower, and how would you tell?
followups.a: Rarely, but coalescing too aggressively can under-parallelize a CPU-bound stage, and the re-planning adds tiny overhead. The Spark UI marks AQE-changed nodes (`AdaptiveSparkPlan isFinalPlan=true`); compare with AQE off. If a stage now runs with too few large tasks, raise `advisoryPartitionSizeInBytes` down or `minPartitionSize`.

followups.q: Does AQE remove the need to broadcast() explicitly?
followups.a: Not entirely. AQE only flips to broadcast when the side's MATERIALIZED shuffle size falls under the adaptive threshold, which means it already paid for one shuffle to find out. An explicit `broadcast()` hint avoids that shuffle from the start, so for a known-small side the hint is still better.

q: Walk through how you read explain(mode='formatted') output to decide whether a query plan is healthy.
a: Read the physical plan bottom-up: scans at the bottom, then exchanges (shuffles), joins, and the final project at the top. Confirm the scan shows PushedFilters and a pruned column list, proving predicate and projection pushdown reached the source; if not, your filter is applied too late. Each Exchange node is a shuffle, so count them: an unexpected Exchange hashpartitioning before a join means a sort-merge join where you wanted a BroadcastExchange. Check the join operator type (BroadcastHashJoin vs SortMergeJoin) and, with AQE on, look for AdaptiveSparkPlan and the final stats; the formatted mode also prints per-node details so you can verify partition counts and filters.
fs: Count Exchange nodes and confirm PushedFilters at the scan; an extra shuffle or SortMergeJoin where you expected a broadcast is the red flag.
fj: Reading the plan top-down or just eyeballing it without checking PushedFilters, join type, and the number of Exchange nodes.

followups.q: With AQE on, the printed plan shows placeholders; how do you see the FINAL plan?
followups.a: Before execution, AQE nodes show as `AdaptiveSparkPlan isFinalPlan=false` with unresolved sub-plans. Run an action first, then call `explain` (or read the SQL tab in the UI), which prints `isFinalPlan=true` with the actually-chosen joins and coalesced partition counts. The pre-execution plan is only a guess.

followups.q: PushedFilters appears but the filter still seems slow; what is the difference between pushed and partition filters?
followups.a: `PushedFilters` are pushed into the file reader for row-group skipping via stats; `PartitionFilters` prune whole directories before reading any file and are far more effective. A predicate on a non-partition, low-selectivity column shows as a pushed filter but still scans most row groups. Check that your hot predicate hits a partition column.

followups.q: What does a ReusedExchange node tell you in the plan?
followups.a: `ReusedExchange` means Spark recognized two subtrees produce the same shuffle and computed it once, reusing the output, e.g. self-joins or a DataFrame used twice. Seeing it is good (saved a shuffle); its absence where you expected reuse hints the subtrees differ subtly and you are paying for the shuffle twice.

q: How does partitioning differ from bucketing in Spark, and when does bucketing eliminate a shuffle that partitioning cannot?
a: partitionBy writes a directory per value of a low-cardinality column (date, region) so reads can prune whole directories, but it does nothing to co-locate join keys and explodes into tiny files on high-cardinality columns. bucketBy(N, key) hash-distributes rows into a fixed N buckets within the table's files based on the join/aggregation key, recording that layout in the metastore. When two tables are bucketed on the same key with the same N, a join needs zero shuffle because matching keys already sit in the same bucket on both sides. The classic pattern is partitionBy("date").bucketBy(64, "user_id"): prune by date, avoid shuffle on user_id; bucketing pays off only for repeated joins/aggs on a high-cardinality key and requires saveAsTable (metastore), not a plain path write.
fs: Bucket on the high-cardinality join key with matching N on both tables to kill the join shuffle; partitionBy only enables directory pruning.
fj: partitionBy on a high-cardinality column (creating millions of tiny dirs), or expecting bucketing to work without matching bucket counts.

code.label: Partition + bucket
lines: # same N both sides -> no shuffle

followups.q: Two tables are bucketed on the same key but one has 64 and the other 32 buckets; does the shuffle-free join still work?
followups.a: Not fully. A shuffle-free join needs identical bucket counts. With 64 vs 32, Spark can sometimes coalesce the 64-bucket side to 32 (if `bucketed scan` coalescing is enabled and one is a multiple of the other), but mismatched non-multiple counts force a re-shuffle. Plan matching N on both tables from the start.

followups.q: Why does adding new data to a bucketed table risk breaking the no-shuffle property?
followups.a: Each insert writes its own set of N bucket files, so a table can accumulate multiple files per bucket. Spark still reads them per bucket, but too many small bucket files hurt, and any operation that re-distributes (like a non-bucket-aware overwrite) can violate the layout. Bucketing favors stable, append-controlled tables.

followups.q: What does the sortBy add on top of bucketBy?
followups.a: `sortBy` orders rows within each bucket file, enabling a sort-merge join to skip the sort step entirely (a `bucketed + sorted` scan), and it improves predicate/min-max skipping inside the bucket. Without it you still avoid the shuffle but pay to sort each bucket at join time.

q: What guarantees does a Structured Streaming checkpoint provide, and what breaks if you delete or relocate it?
a: The checkpoint directory holds the offset log (what was read), the commit log (what was finished), and the state store snapshots; together with replayable sources and idempotent sinks this gives end-to-end exactly-once. On restart, Spark reads the last committed offsets and resumes from there, reprocessing any micro-batch that was in flight but not committed, so no data is lost or double-counted at the sink. Deleting the checkpoint forces the stream to start over from the source's earliest/latest offset and loses all aggregation state, causing duplicates or gaps. You also cannot freely change the query in ways that invalidate the stored state (e.g. altering aggregation keys), and each independent query needs its own checkpoint location.
fs: The checkpoint's offset log + commit log + state store give exactly-once on restart; deleting it loses state and reprocesses from scratch.
fj: Sharing one checkpoint across queries, or assuming you can delete/move it freely without losing state and exactly-once.

followups.q: Exactly-once requires more than the checkpoint; what must the source and sink each provide?
followups.a: The source must be replayable so Spark can re-read uncommitted offsets (Kafka, file source), and the sink must be idempotent or transactional so a reprocessed batch does not double-write (Delta, foreachBatch with upsert). A non-replayable source or a plain append sink degrades the guarantee to at-least-once despite the checkpoint.

followups.q: Which code changes between restarts are safe versus state-breaking with the same checkpoint?
followups.a: Safe: changing filters, projections, sink options, output path. State-breaking: changing aggregation keys, the number/type of stateful operators, output mode, or watermark in incompatible ways, because the stored state no longer maps to the new plan. Those require a new checkpoint and a backfill.

followups.q: Why does each streaming query need its OWN checkpoint location?
followups.a: The offset and commit logs are keyed to one query's source/sink topology. Two queries sharing a directory would clobber each other's offsets and state, corrupting progress tracking and producing duplicates or silent data loss. Always give every `writeStream` a distinct `checkpointLocation`.

q: In Structured Streaming, what does a watermark do for a windowed aggregation, and what happens to data later than the watermark?
a: A watermark with withWatermark("event_time", "10 minutes") tells the engine the maximum lateness it will tolerate, computed as max-seen-event-time minus the threshold. It lets Spark finalize and emit a time window once the watermark passes the window end, and crucially lets it drop the window's state afterward so state does not grow unbounded. Events arriving with an event_time older than the current watermark are considered too late and are dropped from the aggregation, not added. Watermarks are required for append-mode windowed aggregations and for bounding state in stream-stream joins; pick the threshold from real observed lateness, since too tight drops valid data and too loose bloats state.
fs: The watermark bounds state and emits/expires windows; events older than it are dropped, so tune the threshold to real lateness.
fj: Omitting a watermark on a windowed agg (unbounded state growth) or assuming late data is silently still included.

followups.q: Why does append output mode specifically require a watermark on a windowed aggregation?
followups.a: Append mode emits a row only once it is final and will never change. Without a watermark, Spark cannot know when a window stops receiving late updates, so it can never declare a result final and would emit nothing. The watermark defines the cutoff after which the window is closed and safe to append.

followups.q: How does the watermark behave across multiple stateful operators in one query?
followups.a: Spark tracks a single global watermark and, by default, uses the MINIMUM event-time across all input streams/operators so no operator drops data another still needs. You can switch to max policy via `spark.sql.streaming.multipleWatermarkPolicy`, but min is the safe default to avoid prematurely expiring state.

followups.q: In a stream-stream join, what does the watermark bound that it does not in a single aggregation?
followups.a: It bounds how long each side's unmatched rows are retained waiting for a match on the other stream, plus a join-time-range condition. Without watermarks (and a time constraint) the join state grows forever because Spark must keep every row in case a future match arrives. The watermark lets it evict rows past the allowed lateness.

q: Write a Delta Lake MERGE that upserts a CDC batch, and explain how to keep it from rewriting the whole table.
a: MERGE matches the target to a source on a key, then runs WHEN MATCHED THEN UPDATE for changed rows and WHEN NOT MATCHED THEN INSERT for new ones, all atomically. The performance trap is that Delta must rewrite every data file that contains a matched row, so a key-only match condition can touch the entire table. Add a partition predicate to the ON clause (for example t.dt = s.dt) so Delta prunes to just the relevant partitions and rewrites far fewer files, and keep the source small enough to broadcast so dynamic file pruning kicks in. For deletes-in-CDC, add WHEN MATCHED AND s.op = 'D' THEN DELETE; periodically run OPTIMIZE since MERGE produces new small files.
fs: Add a partition predicate to the MERGE ON clause so Delta prunes files; key-only matches can rewrite the whole table.
fj: Writing MERGE on the key alone and wondering why it rewrites everything, or forgetting MERGE leaves small files needing OPTIMIZE.

code.label: Pruned upsert
lines: WHEN MATCHED AND s.op='D' THEN DELETE

followups.q: If the CDC batch has multiple updates for the same key, why does the MERGE fail?
followups.a: Delta raises an error when a single target row matches more than one source row, because the update result would be ambiguous. Pre-dedupe the source to one row per key (keep the latest by sequence/timestamp via `row_number`) before the MERGE so each target matches at most one source row.

followups.q: How does Delta's MERGE achieve atomicity if it rewrites files in place?
followups.a: It does not edit files in place: MERGE writes new data files, then atomically commits a new version in the `_delta_log` transaction log that swaps which files are active. Readers see either the old or new snapshot, never a partial state. Failed merges leave orphan files that VACUUM later cleans up.

followups.q: What is the difference between adding t.dt = s.dt to the ON clause versus a WHERE on the source?
followups.a: A predicate in the ON clause lets Delta perform file/partition pruning on the TARGET, skipping files it need not rewrite. Filtering only the source narrows what you scan but Delta may still scan/rewrite all target partitions to find non-matches. The target-side predicate is what cuts the write amplification.

q: A new upstream field appears and your nightly write fails with a schema mismatch; how do you handle schema evolution safely in Delta?
a: By default Delta rejects writes whose schema differs from the table to protect downstream consumers. To accept additive changes (new columns), use .option("mergeSchema", "true") on that specific write so Delta adds the columns and backfills existing rows with null, or for a MERGE set spark.databricks.delta.schema.autoMerge.enabled=true. Prefer scoping mergeSchema to the write rather than the global autoMerge flag, because the global setting silently absorbs any stray column across all jobs and lets typos pollute the schema. mergeSchema handles adds and safe widening; it will not silently change a column's type incompatibly, and dropping or renaming requires an explicit ALTER with column mapping or an overwriteSchema.
fs: Scope mergeSchema to the one write for additive evolution; the global autoMerge flag silently absorbs typos table-wide.
fj: Turning on global autoMerge everywhere (so typos become columns), or expecting mergeSchema to handle incompatible type changes.

code.label: Additive evolution
lines: # new cols added; old rows -> null

followups.q: An upstream column changes from int to string; will mergeSchema handle it?
followups.a: No. `mergeSchema` only allows additive changes and safe widening (e.g. int to long), never an incompatible narrowing or type swap like int to string. That write still fails. You must cast explicitly to a compatible type or do an `overwriteSchema` rewrite, which rewrites the whole table's metadata and data for that column.

followups.q: Why is dropping or renaming a column harder than adding one in Delta?
followups.a: Adds are append-only metadata. Drop/rename without column mapping would invalidate the physical Parquet column names that existing files reference. You must enable `delta.columnMapping.mode = name` first, which decouples logical names from physical ones, then `ALTER TABLE ... RENAME/DROP COLUMN` becomes a cheap metadata operation.

followups.q: What is the operational risk of leaving global autoMerge on permanently?
followups.a: Every job that writes the table can silently introduce columns, so a typo (`amountt`), a mis-mapped field, or a bad upstream change pollutes the schema permanently and bloats files with sparse columns. Scoping `mergeSchema` per intentional write keeps evolution a deliberate, reviewed act rather than an accident.

q: Your streaming sink produces thousands of tiny files per hour, killing query performance; how do you compact them?
a: Small files force the reader to open many file handles and list huge directories, so scans and the driver's planning both slow down. On Delta, run OPTIMIZE, which uses a bin-packing algorithm to rewrite many small files into right-sized ones (default target ~1 GB), and add ZORDER BY on common filter columns to co-locate related data for better data skipping. For continuous streaming you can enable auto-compaction (optimizeWrite / autoCompact) so Delta compacts after each commit instead of leaving the mess. On plain Parquet without Delta, the manual equivalent is reading the partition and rewriting with coalesce or repartition to fewer, larger files. After compaction, run VACUUM to remove the now-unreferenced small files past the retention window.
fs: Run OPTIMIZE (bin-packing, ~1 GB targets) plus ZORDER, or enable auto-compaction; VACUUM clears the orphaned small files.
fj: Ignoring the small-file problem until scans crawl, or running VACUUM with too-short retention and breaking time travel.

followups.q: Why does VACUUM with a short retention break time travel and concurrent reads?
followups.a: VACUUM physically deletes data files no longer referenced by the current version but still needed by older versions and by in-flight reads. A retention below the default 7 days can delete files a long-running query or a time-travel `versionAsOf` still references, causing `FileNotFound`. Keep retention above your longest reader plus desired history.

followups.q: How does ZORDER differ from partitionBy for speeding up filtered queries?
followups.a: `partitionBy` physically separates data into directories prunable before reads but only works for low-cardinality columns. `ZORDER` co-locates related rows of high-cardinality columns within files using a space-filling curve so min/max stats skip more row groups. They are complementary: partition by date, ZORDER by user_id.

followups.q: Does optimizeWrite/autoCompact remove the need for periodic OPTIMIZE?
followups.a: Largely for steady streaming, since they compact at write time, but they target a smaller file size and only handle data written in that commit. Periodic `OPTIMIZE` with ZORDER is still worthwhile to reach the larger ~1GB targets and to cluster across many commits. Auto-features reduce, not eliminate, scheduled compaction.

q: You re-run a daily job for one partition but it wipes the entire table; what is dynamic partition overwrite and how do you use it correctly?
a: With the default mode='overwrite', Spark replaces the WHOLE table (or, for partitioned writes, may clear unrelated partitions), so a one-day rerun destroys history. Setting spark.sql.sources.partitionOverwriteMode=dynamic (or the writer option partitionOverwriteMode=dynamic) makes overwrite replace only the partitions actually present in the incoming DataFrame and leave all others untouched, which is exactly what an idempotent daily backfill needs. On Delta, the targeted alternative is replaceWhere with an explicit predicate, which atomically overwrites only rows matching the condition; note you cannot combine replaceWhere and partitionOverwriteMode in the same write, and replaceWhere takes precedence. Always confirm the partition column is in the DataFrame, or dynamic mode has nothing to scope to.
fs: Set partitionOverwriteMode=dynamic (or use Delta replaceWhere) so an overwrite replaces only the incoming partitions, not the whole table.
fj: Running mode('overwrite') on a partitioned table and nuking every other partition, not knowing dynamic mode or replaceWhere exists.

code.label: Dynamic overwrite
lines: # only dt's in df are replaced

followups.q: Dynamic overwrite replaces only present partitions; what failure mode does that introduce on its own?
followups.a: It silently leaves stale data in partitions NOT present in the incoming batch. If a backfill should have emptied a partition (all rows deleted upstream), dynamic mode keeps the old rows because that `dt` is absent from the DataFrame. For true deletes you need an explicit overwrite of that partition or Delta `replaceWhere`.

followups.q: Why is Delta replaceWhere safer than dynamic partition overwrite for correctness?
followups.a: `replaceWhere('dt = ...')` is a single atomic transaction validated against the predicate: Spark errors if any incoming row falls outside the predicate, preventing accidental cross-partition writes. Dynamic mode infers scope from the data with no guardrail, so a stray partition value in the DataFrame silently overwrites an unintended partition.

followups.q: What happens if the partition column is missing from the DataFrame under dynamic mode?
followups.a: Dynamic mode scopes the overwrite by the distinct partition values present in the data; with no partition column there are no partition values to scope to, so it falls back to behaving like a static full overwrite or errors. Always confirm the `partitionBy` column is actually in the DataFrame before the write.

q: In a long iterative PySpark job the DAG keeps growing and recovery is slow; what does checkpoint() do that cache() does not?
a: cache()/persist() keep the partitions but also keep the full lineage, so on executor loss Spark must recompute from the source, and in iterative algorithms the lineage chain grows until the planner itself becomes slow or stack-overflows. df.checkpoint() (after setting spark.sparkContext.setCheckpointDir) materializes the DataFrame to reliable storage like HDFS/S3 and TRUNCATES the lineage, so recovery reads the checkpoint instead of replaying hundreds of stages. It is an action (eager by default) and writes to durable storage, unlike the in-memory, lineage-preserving cache; there is also localCheckpoint() which is faster but not fault-tolerant. Use checkpoint to break lineage in deep iterative or recursive pipelines, and cache for plain reuse where lineage is short.
fs: checkpoint() writes to reliable storage and truncates lineage; cache() keeps lineage and only recomputes, so deep iterative jobs need checkpoint.
fj: Using cache() to fix a runaway lineage, not realizing only checkpoint() truncates it and requires a checkpoint dir.

followups.q: Should you cache() a DataFrame before checkpoint()ing it, and why?
followups.a: Yes. `checkpoint` is an action that recomputes the DataFrame to write it, and if downstream code uses the DataFrame again it recomputes once more. Calling `cache()` first means the checkpoint write reads from cache and later uses hit cache too, avoiding a double computation of the whole lineage.

followups.q: When is localCheckpoint() acceptable despite not being fault-tolerant?
followups.a: `localCheckpoint` truncates lineage by writing to executor local disk/cache, which is much faster than reliable storage but lost if the executor dies. It is fine when you only need to break a runaway lineage for planner performance within a job and can tolerate recomputation on the rare node failure, e.g. exploratory or short iterative loops.

followups.q: Why doesn't cache() solve the stack-overflow-in-planner problem on deep lineages?
followups.a: Cache stores materialized partitions but leaves the logical/physical lineage intact, so Catalyst still has to build and analyze the full multi-hundred-node plan tree, which is what overflows the recursion or slows planning. Only `checkpoint` cuts the lineage so the plan tree restarts from the materialized node.

q: What is the difference between select, withColumn, and selectExpr, and why can chaining many withColumn calls hurt?
a: select projects a chosen set of columns and is the natural way to add several derived columns in one pass. withColumn adds or replaces exactly one column and returns a new DataFrame, so chaining dozens of them builds a deeply nested projection that can blow up Catalyst analysis time and plan size. selectExpr lets you write SQL-style expression strings, handy for concise casts and CASE logic. For many derived columns, prefer a single select with a list of expressions (or withColumns in newer Spark) instead of a long withColumn chain, which keeps the logical plan flat and analysis fast.
fs: Build many derived columns in one select (or withColumns), not a long withColumn chain that bloats the Catalyst plan.
fj: Chaining 30 withColumn calls and hitting slow analysis, unaware that one select with a list is flatter and faster.

followups.q: Does the deep withColumn plan actually run slower, or only analyze slower?
followups.a: Mostly it ANALYZES slower: each `withColumn` wraps a new Project node, so Catalyst builds and re-resolves a tower of projections, sometimes seconds of driver-side planning before any task runs. The optimized physical plan usually collapses adjacent projects, so runtime is often fine; the pain is plan compilation, not execution.

followups.q: When is selectExpr genuinely better than select with column objects?
followups.a: `selectExpr` shines for terse SQL-string logic, dynamic column lists built from strings, or porting SQL CASE expressions, e.g. `selectExpr('id', 'amt*1.07 as t', 'CASE WHEN ...')`. It avoids importing functions and is concise, but loses Python-side type checking and IDE help, so prefer column objects when the logic is built programmatically.

followups.q: How does withColumns (plural) in newer Spark fix the chaining problem?
followups.a: `withColumns({'a': expr1, 'b': expr2})` (Spark 3.3+) adds multiple columns in a SINGLE Project node, giving the flat plan of a `select` while keeping the convenient add-column semantics. It is the direct replacement for a long `withColumn` chain when you want to keep all existing columns.

q: How do inner, left, and anti joins differ in PySpark, and which would you use to find records present in A but missing in B?
a: An inner join keeps only rows whose key matches on both sides. A left (left_outer) join keeps every row from the left and fills right-side columns with null where there is no match. A left_anti join is the right tool to find rows in A with no match in B: it returns only the unmatched left rows and adds no columns from B, which is cleaner and cheaper than a left join followed by filtering where the right key is null. There is also left_semi, the mirror, which keeps left rows that DO have a match, again without importing B's columns.
fs: Use left_anti to get rows in A missing from B in one step, instead of a left join plus an isNull filter.
fj: Doing a left join then filtering right-key IS NULL, or confusing left_anti (no match) with left_semi (has match).

code.label: Rows in A not in B
lines: # only A rows with no B match

followups.q: Why can a left join + IS NULL filter give WRONG counts where left_anti does not?
followups.a: If the right side has duplicate keys, the left join fan-outs each left row into multiple matched rows; the `IS NULL` filter then only catches genuinely unmatched ones, but you have already changed the row multiplicity for matched keys upstream. `left_anti` returns each left row at most once with no fan-out, so it is correct and cheaper.

followups.q: How does a NULL value in the join key behave across these join types?
followups.a: A NULL key never equals another NULL under standard equality, so NULL-keyed left rows are treated as unmatched: they survive `left_anti` and `left_outer` (with null right cols) but are dropped by inner and `left_semi`. If you need NULL=NULL matching, use `eqNullSafe` (`<=>`) in the join condition.

followups.q: Does left_semi let you reference B's columns in a later select?
followups.a: No. `left_semi` only filters left rows by existence of a match and projects ONLY the left schema, so B's columns are not available afterward. If you need values from B, use an inner join instead and accept the potential fan-out (and dedupe B first if needed).

q: How do you express a multi-branch conditional column in PySpark, and why is when/otherwise better than a Python UDF?
a: Use F.when(cond, val) chained with more .when() calls and a final .otherwise(default), which compiles to a native CASE WHEN that Catalyst can optimize and codegen entirely in the JVM. A Python UDF for the same logic runs row-by-row across the JVM-to-Python boundary with serialization overhead and blocks predicate pushdown. Without a trailing .otherwise(), unmatched rows return null, which is a common silent bug. Keep branch logic in when/otherwise, regexp, and arithmetic expressions so the whole thing stays a single optimized stage.
fs: when/otherwise compiles to a native CASE WHEN with codegen; a UDF for the same branching is slower and opaque to Catalyst.
fj: Reaching for a Python UDF to bucket values, or forgetting the .otherwise() so unmatched rows silently become null.

code.label: Bucket with when/otherwise
lines: from pyspark.sql import functions as F

followups.q: Why does the ORDER of the when branches matter, and what bug does swapping them cause?
followups.a: `when` evaluates top-down and takes the FIRST true branch, so a broader condition placed before a narrower one shadows it. Putting `amt > 10` before `amt > 100` would label every high amount 'silver' because the first match wins. Order branches from most specific/highest threshold to least.

followups.q: How do you handle NULL in the condition column so it doesn't silently fall to otherwise?
followups.a: A `when(F.col('amt') > 100, ...)` evaluates to NULL (not true) when `amt` is NULL, so the row drops through to `.otherwise()`, possibly mislabeling it. Add an explicit `F.when(F.col('amt').isNull(), 'unknown')` first so NULLs are handled deliberately rather than lumped into the default.

followups.q: Is there a more concise native option than a long when chain for value-to-value mapping?
followups.a: For a fixed lookup, build a map literal with `F.create_map` and index it, or `F.coalesce` over equality checks; for ranges, `when/otherwise` stays clearest. A small broadcast lookup join also works for large mapping tables. All keep the logic in the JVM, unlike a UDF dictionary lookup.

q: Why does df['x'] == null never match rows in PySpark, and how do you correctly test for and fill nulls?
a: Spark uses three-valued logic, so any comparison with null (including = null) returns null, not true, and a filter keeps only rows that evaluate to true. To test for null use col.isNull() / col.isNotNull(), not == None. To compare two columns treating null-equals-null as a match, use eqNullSafe (the <=> operator). To replace nulls, coalesce(col, default) returns the first non-null argument, and df.na.fill({...}) or fillna sets defaults per column. These keep null logic explicit instead of accidentally dropping rows.
fs: Comparisons with null yield null under three-valued logic; use isNull/isNotNull, eqNullSafe, coalesce, and na.fill.
fj: Writing col == None and getting zero rows, or assuming a filter keeps null rows.

followups.q: Under three-valued logic, does NOT (x = 5) include rows where x is NULL?
followups.a: No. If `x` is NULL, `x = 5` is NULL, and `NOT NULL` is still NULL, which a filter treats as not-true, so NULL rows are dropped by both `x = 5` and `NOT (x = 5)`. To include NULLs you must add an explicit `OR x IS NULL`. This asymmetry surprises people expecting NULLs in the negation.

followups.q: How does df.na.fill behave when you pass a single value versus a per-column dict?
followups.a: `df.na.fill(0)` fills ALL numeric columns (and `fill('')` all string columns) of the matching type, which can clobber columns you did not mean to touch. A dict `na.fill({'sid':'NA','amt':0})` scopes the fill per column. Prefer the dict form to avoid accidentally filling unintended columns.

followups.q: When would eqNullSafe change the result of a join versus a normal equality join?
followups.a: A normal join on a key drops rows where the key is NULL on either side (NULL never equals NULL). `a.join(b, a.k.eqNullSafe(b.k))` treats NULL=NULL as a match, so NULL-keyed rows pair up. Use it when NULL is a meaningful, joinable category rather than missing data.

q: When should you pass an explicit schema to spark.read instead of letting Spark infer it, and how do you build one?
a: Define a StructType of StructField(name, type, nullable) entries and pass it via .schema() for CSV/JSON reads. Inference triggers an extra pass over the data to sample types, which is slow on large inputs and non-deterministic when columns are sometimes empty, so an explicit schema is faster and prevents a column from being read as the wrong type (e.g. a zip code as a long that drops leading zeros). Parquet and ORC carry their schema in the footer, so inference there is cheap and explicit schemas matter mainly for evolution control. An explicit schema also lets you reject malformed rows predictably.
fs: An explicit StructType skips the inference scan, fixes types deterministically, and avoids cost like reading zip codes as longs.
fj: Relying on inferSchema on a huge CSV, paying an extra scan and risking wrong/unstable column types.

followups.q: With an explicit schema, what happens to a row whose value doesn't fit the declared type?
followups.a: It depends on the read `mode`: `PERMISSIVE` (default) sets the bad field to NULL and can capture the raw row in `columnNameOfCorruptRecord`; `DROPMALFORMED` discards the row; `FAILFAST` aborts the job. So an explicit schema plus `FAILFAST` is how you reject malformed input predictably instead of silently nulling.

followups.q: Does providing a schema for Parquet do anything, given Parquet stores its own?
followups.a: Parquet's footer already defines types, so a supplied schema is used mainly to SELECT/reorder columns and to enforce expected evolution; mismatched types can error or be coerced. The big win of explicit schemas is for schemaless formats (CSV/JSON); for Parquet it is about column selection and validation, not avoiding an inference scan.

followups.q: Why is samplingRatio-based inference non-deterministic across runs?
followups.a: Inference samples a fraction of rows (or the first file) to guess types, so a column that is integer-looking in the sampled rows but has a decimal or string later can be typed differently on another run or partition order. An explicit schema removes that run-to-run variance entirely.

q: How do you flatten a nested struct and explode an array column in PySpark, and what does explode do to row count?
a: Access struct fields with dot notation or col('addr.city'), or call select('addr.*') to promote every nested field to a top-level column. For an array column, explode(col) emits one output row per element, multiplying row count by the array length and dropping rows whose array is empty or null. Use explode_outer when you must keep rows with null/empty arrays, and posexplode when you need the element index. For a map column, explode yields key and value columns. This is the standard pattern for flattening JSON-shaped data into a tabular form.
fs: select('struct.*') flattens nested fields; explode emits a row per array element and drops empty/null arrays unless you use explode_outer.
fj: Expecting explode to preserve rows with empty/null arrays, or not knowing explode_outer and posexplode exist.

followups.q: What error do you hit if you put two explode() calls in the same select, and how do you fix it?
followups.a: Spark allows only one generator (explode) per `select`/projection and raises an `Only one generator allowed` analysis error for two. Chain them: explode the first array, then a second `select`/`withColumn` to explode the next, producing the cross-product of both arrays across separate steps.

followups.q: How big can the row blow-up get, and why is that a performance hazard?
followups.a: `explode` multiplies rows by array length per row, so an average array of 50 turns 1M rows into 50M, exploding shuffle, join, and memory cost downstream. A few rows with pathologically large arrays also create skew. Filter or aggregate before exploding, or cap array sizes, to avoid a runaway fan-out.

followups.q: When would you flatten a struct with explicit aliased fields instead of select('addr.*')?
followups.a: Use explicit `col('addr.city').alias('addr_city')` when `addr.*` would collide with existing top-level names, when you need a prefix to avoid ambiguity, or when you only want a subset of nested fields. `addr.*` is convenient but promotes every field with its bare name, which can clash or pull in unwanted columns.

q: Show several aggregations in one groupBy().agg() and explain why collect_list and countDistinct deserve caution.
a: Pass multiple aggregate expressions to a single agg() call, each aliased, so the whole rollup runs in one shuffle: F.sum, F.avg, F.count, F.countDistinct, F.max all together. countDistinct is expensive because exact distinct counting needs a global shuffle; approx_count_distinct (HyperLogLog) is far cheaper when an estimate is acceptable. collect_list / collect_set gather all values per group into an array on one task, so a skewed hot key can OOM that executor. Always alias outputs since the default generated names like sum(amt) are awkward downstream.
fs: Batch all aggregates in one agg() call; prefer approx_count_distinct over countDistinct, and watch collect_list OOM on skewed groups.
fj: Calling agg multiple times (extra passes), or using collect_list on a hot key and OOMing one executor.

followups.q: What accuracy does approx_count_distinct give, and how do you control it?
followups.a: It uses HyperLogLog with a default relative standard deviation around 5%; you pass a smaller `rsd` (e.g. 0.01 for ~1%) at the cost of more memory and CPU. It is ideal for dashboards and cardinality monitoring where a small error is fine; use exact `countDistinct` only when the precise number legally/financially matters.

followups.q: Why doesn't Spark do a partial (map-side) pre-aggregation for collect_list like it does for sum?
followups.a: `sum`/`count` are algebraic, so Spark combines partial results per partition before the shuffle, shrinking data moved. `collect_list` must concatenate every value, so there is no reducing partial form: all values for a key travel to one reducer and pile onto one task, which is exactly why a hot key OOMs.

followups.q: How would you safely get the top-N values per group instead of collect_list of everything?
followups.a: Avoid materializing the whole list: use a `row_number()`/`rank()` window partitioned by the group and filter to N, or `slice(sort_array(collect_list(...)), 1, N)` only when groups are small. The windowed approach bounds per-task memory and is the standard skew-safe way to get top-N per key.

q: What is the difference between an Arrow-optimized Python UDF and a pandas_udf in modern Spark, and when do you pick each?
a: A pandas_udf (since Spark 2.3) is vectorized: Spark ships Arrow batches to the worker and your function operates on a whole pandas Series at once, ideal when the logic is naturally vectorized or calls a pandas/numpy/sklearn routine. An Arrow-optimized Python UDF (Spark 3.5, enabled with useArrow=True or spark.sql.execution.pythonUDF.arrow.enabled) still runs row-by-row in Python but uses Arrow for serialization and consistent type coercion, roughly 1.6x faster than a pickled UDF without rewriting to pandas. Reach for pandas_udf when you can vectorize; use an Arrow UDF as a cheap drop-in speedup for existing per-row UDFs. Native Spark expressions still beat both when the logic can be expressed without Python.
fs: pandas_udf vectorizes over a Series via Arrow; an Arrow-optimized UDF is still row-wise but ~1.6x faster than pickled via Arrow serialization.
fj: Thinking useArrow makes a UDF vectorized (it doesn't — still row-by-row), or that pandas_udf and Arrow UDF are the same thing.

followups.q: Beyond speed, what type-handling problem does the Arrow-optimized UDF fix versus a pickled UDF?
followups.a: Pickled UDFs rely on Python's loose coercion, so a returned value can silently mis-cast or lose precision (e.g. int vs float, timestamp tz). The Arrow path enforces consistent, columnar type coercion matching the declared return type, catching mismatches more predictably. It is as much a correctness/consistency win as a perf win.

followups.q: If a pandas_udf is faster, why ever use an Arrow-optimized row UDF?
followups.a: When the existing logic is inherently per-row and awkward to vectorize (complex branching, calling a row-oriented library), `useArrow=True` is a one-flag drop-in that speeds it ~1.6x with zero rewrite. Rewriting to a `pandas_udf` Series operation may be more effort or unnatural for that logic.

followups.q: Does enabling Arrow have prerequisites or failure modes to watch?
followups.a: It needs a compatible `pyarrow` installed on driver and workers; on unsupported types or version mismatch Spark falls back to non-Arrow with a warning, silently losing the speedup. Some complex/nested types are not Arrow-serializable. Verify `pyarrow` is present and check logs for fallback warnings.

q: What is pandas API on Spark (pyspark.pandas) and how does it differ from calling toPandas()?
a: pyspark.pandas (import pyspark.pandas as ps) gives a pandas-like API whose operations compile to Spark jobs and run distributed across the cluster, so it scales past a single machine's RAM while keeping familiar pandas syntax. toPandas() instead collects the entire DataFrame to the driver as a real pandas object, bounded by driver memory and a common OOM source. Use pandas-on-Spark to port pandas code at scale or let analysts stay in pandas idioms; use toPandas() only on a small, already-reduced result. Note pandas-on-Spark has subtle differences (default index handling, ordering) and not every pandas method is supported.
fs: pyspark.pandas runs pandas-style ops distributed as Spark jobs; toPandas() pulls everything to the driver and is RAM-bounded.
fj: Using toPandas() on a big DataFrame to 'use pandas', OOMing the driver instead of pyspark.pandas which stays distributed.

followups.q: Why is the default index in pandas-on-Spark a hidden performance trap?
followups.a: Pandas needs a global row order; the default `sequence` index requires collecting everything to one partition to assign monotonic ids, killing parallelism. Set `compute.default_index_type` to `distributed` or `distributed-sequence` so the index is computed without a single-partition bottleneck, at the cost of non-consecutive ids.

followups.q: Which pandas operations should you expect to be slow or unsupported on pandas-on-Spark?
followups.a: Order-dependent and row-position ops (`iloc` by position, `shift` without ordering, cumulative ops needing a global order) are expensive because Spark is unordered, and some pandas methods are simply not implemented. Anything that implies materializing global order or per-row Python execution should be reviewed before relying on it at scale.

followups.q: Is there overhead converting between a pandas-on-Spark frame and a regular Spark DataFrame?
followups.a: Conversion is cheap/metadata-level via `ps.DataFrame.to_spark()` and `df.pandas_api()` because both wrap the same underlying Spark plan; no data is collected. So you can drop into native Spark for a join, then back to the pandas API, mixing idioms without paying a collect.

q: Why is calling collect() (or toPandas()) on a large DataFrame dangerous, and what are the safer alternatives?
a: collect() and toPandas() pull every partition back to the single driver JVM, whose memory is far smaller than the executors combined, so a large result triggers driver OOM and serialization overhead even when the cluster could handle the data fine. To preview, use df.show(n) or df.take(n), which fetch only a few rows. To inspect more without blowing memory, df.toLocalIterator() streams one partition at a time. If you genuinely need every row out, write to storage and read elsewhere instead of routing it through the driver. The rule is to reduce/aggregate first and only collect a small final result.
fs: collect/toPandas funnel all data through the driver (OOM); use show/take to peek, toLocalIterator to stream, or write to storage.
fj: Calling collect() to 'see the data' on a multi-GB DataFrame and crashing the driver, when show/take would do.

lines: handle(r)  # one partition at a time

followups.q: toLocalIterator streams one partition at a time, but what can still OOM the driver?
followups.a: A single oversized partition: `toLocalIterator` brings one full partition into driver memory at a time, so if one partition is huge (skew, few large partitions), it still OOMs. Repartition to smaller, even partitions first so no single one exceeds driver heap.

followups.q: Besides driver OOM, what other limit can collect() hit even on a moderate result?
followups.a: `spark.driver.maxResultSize` caps the total serialized size of results returned to the driver (default 1g); exceeding it aborts the job with an explicit error rather than silently OOMing. It is a guardrail you can raise, but hitting it is a sign you should aggregate or write to storage instead.

followups.q: If you must get all rows out of Spark, what is the right pattern instead of collect?
followups.a: Write the DataFrame to distributed storage (Parquet/Delta) with executors writing in parallel, then read it where you need it. This keeps data off the driver entirely and parallelizes the I/O. Routing every row through `collect`/`toPandas` serializes the whole cluster's output through one machine.

q: How do you unit test PySpark transformation code, and what makes a good test setup?
a: Factor transformations into pure functions that take a DataFrame and return a DataFrame, so you can feed tiny in-memory inputs built with spark.createDataFrame and assert on the output. Share one local SparkSession across the test module via a session-scoped pytest fixture (master('local[1]')) because session startup is slow; tear it down with spark.stop(). Compare results with pyspark.testing.assertDataFrameEqual (built into Spark 3.5+) or chispa's assert_df_equality, which check schema and rows and print readable diffs. Keep fixtures small and deterministic, and assert on both schema and values so a silent type change is caught.
fs: Test pure DataFrame-in/DataFrame-out functions on tiny inputs with a session-scoped local Spark fixture and assertDataFrameEqual.
fj: Spinning up a new SparkSession per test (slow), or eyeballing show() output instead of asserting schema and rows.

followups.q: Row order is nondeterministic; how does assertDataFrameEqual handle that and when does it not?
followups.a: `assertDataFrameEqual` has been updated to compare rows ignoring order by default in recent versions, but to be safe sort both sides (or pass the option) before comparing, since shuffles produce arbitrary order. Asserting on order only makes sense when the transformation itself includes a deterministic final `orderBy`.

followups.q: Why use local[1] over local[*] in tests, and what is the tradeoff?
followups.a: `local[1]` makes execution single-threaded and deterministic, surfacing logic bugs without parallelism noise and avoiding flaky ordering. The tradeoff is it will not catch partition-boundary bugs (e.g. assuming all rows in one partition). Use `local[1]` for most logic tests and a small `local[2]` case when partition behavior matters.

followups.q: How do you keep the slow SparkSession startup from dominating a large test suite?
followups.a: Scope the fixture to `session` (one JVM for the whole run) and configure `spark.sql.shuffle.partitions` low (e.g. 1-4) so tiny test data does not spawn 200 empty shuffle tasks. Disabling AQE and UI in tests also trims overhead. The goal is one Spark startup amortized across all tests.

q: Give a complete window-function pattern for keeping only the latest record per key, and explain why it beats a self-join.
a: Define Window.partitionBy(key).orderBy(desc(ts)), add row_number().over(w), then filter rn == 1 to keep the most recent row per key in a single pass. This is the canonical deduplication/SCD-latest pattern and is cleaner than a groupBy(key).agg(max(ts)) followed by a self-join back to the original, which shuffles twice and can re-duplicate on ties. row_number breaks ties deterministically (one row) where rank/dense_rank would keep all tied rows. The window still shuffles by key, but it is one shuffle and preserves all columns.
fs: row_number over partitionBy(key).orderBy(desc(ts)) then filter rn==1 gets latest-per-key in one shuffle, no self-join.
fj: Doing max(ts) then a self-join (double shuffle, tie duplicates), or using rank which keeps tied rows instead of one.

followups.q: Two records share the same key and identical max ts; how do you make row_number deterministic?
followups.a: With a tie, `row_number` picks an arbitrary survivor that can change across runs. Add a secondary tiebreaker to the `orderBy`, e.g. `desc('ts'), desc('version')` or a unique id, so the choice is stable and reproducible. Deterministic ordering is essential for idempotent reruns.

followups.q: For very high-cardinality keys, is dropDuplicates(subset) cheaper than the row_number window?
followups.a: `dropDuplicates([key])` can be cheaper because it does a hash-based dedup without a full per-partition sort, but it gives a NONDETERMINISTIC survivor, so it is only safe when any row per key is acceptable. When you need the latest specifically, the `row_number` window is required despite the sort cost.

followups.q: How would this pattern extend to building a full SCD Type 2 history instead of just latest?
followups.a: Instead of filtering `rn == 1`, use `lead(ts).over(w)` to compute each record's `valid_to` from the next record's timestamp (NULL/high-date for the current row), and mark `is_current = rn == 1`. That turns the same window into effective-dated history rows rather than collapsing to the latest.

q: When is dropping from the DataFrame API down to the RDD API actually justified, and what do you lose?
